clean_story:
Stretched memories. Shrunken timelines.

Nostalgia comes from nostos (homecoming) and algos (pain)—a bittersweet ache for the past. It doesn’t just color memories; it warps time like a funhouse mirror.

Here’s how: Your brain stores routine moments (commuting, chores) as blurry snapshots, but novel or emotional events (first kisses, graduations) get high-definition encoding. When you recall a nostalgic year—say, age 12—your mind replays only the vivid scenes, skipping the 347 boring days in between. Like flipping through a photo album where 90% of the pages are blank, yet your brain stitches the gaps into a seamless "era." The more you revisit these highlights, the more your memory compresses the timeline, making a decade feel like a summer.

Example: Studies show people over 40 often recall their 20s as "just yesterday," while the same span from 30–40 feels twice as long. The culprit? Nostalgia’s editing. Your 20s are packed with firsts (jobs, love, failures), so they’re stored as dense, retrieval-ready clips. Later years, with fewer novelties, get filed as thin summaries.

Three related subjects are memory distortion tricks, why childhood summers felt endless, and how music hijacks time perception.
